claim: agents who conceal information are judged more negatively than those who disclose, despite the concealer's belief otherwise.

phenomenon: negative evaluation of norm-violating group members
claim: preference for certainty drives negative evaluation of individuals who deviate from group stereotypes, independent of the stereotype's truth.
stereotypes reflecting presumed environmental conditions override group-based stereotypes when explicit ecological information is available.
memory for one's own unethical actions becomes progressively less clear over time, enabling repeated transgression.
framing rule-enforcement through relational concern rather than punishment reduces defiance and improves behavioral compliance.
actors choose not to reveal or minimize deliberation when choosing to help others, using this unreflective stance as a trustworthiness signal.

phenomenon: misalignment between procedural salience and outcome satisfaction
claim: recipients care less about procedural fairness than allocators predict because the outcome dominates attention after allocation.
subjective experience of low social position triggers increased appetite and caloric consumption, mimicking ecological scarcity response.
backward-looking comparisons activate concern in groups normally resistant to forward-looking framings, reducing ideological polarization.

phenomenon: incentive-induced mindset shift and motivation reduction
claim: tangible incentives redirect attention from intrinsic goals to cost-benefit calculus, reducing prosocial behavior among high-intrinsic-motivation agents.
observers' selection and quality-judgment of communicated information depend on communicator appearance, introducing systematic bias.
belief in free agency strengthens attribution of behavior to internal rather than external causes, increasing prescribed punishment.
whether norm-enforcement relies on adaptive learning or reflexive response depends on the strategic payoff structure of the interaction.
reducing perceivers' attribution of personhood increases willingness to inflict harm when instrumentally useful, but not when morally justified.
exposure to rising inequality increases belief in structural barriers and support for redistribution policy.

phenomenon: ephemerality and disinhibition in digital disclosure
claim: temporary-storage affordances increase disclosure risk by reducing privacy concern, but observers' impressions depend on revealed content, not storage duration.
comparative feature-based search promotes lower time-discounting than integrative within-option search, and is amenable to low-friction intervention.
observers overestimate progress toward equity, driven by belief in distributive justice and social exposure, creating unfounded optimism.
training in reframing techniques shifts valuation and choice toward delayed gratification, with effects generalizing beyond trained items.

phenomenon: social-learning and partisan-framing in belief update
claim: exposure to diverse beliefs in structured networks improves accuracy unless partisan identity is made salient, which sustains polarization.
